title: Realizing leakage elimination operator-based adiabatic speedup on a superconducting quantum processor
authors: Jia-Yi Fan, Kai-Yu Yuan, Zong-Yuan Ge, Feng-Hua Ren, Zhao-Ming Wang
abstract: The slow evolution required for adiabaticity in adiabatic quantum computation renders the system vulnerable to environmental noise. Leakage elimination operator (LEO) control provides an effective strategy to realize adiabatic speedup over a short timescale, thus mitigating the noise impact. Despite extensive theoretical investigations, the realization of LEO-based adiabatic speedup on realistic superconducting quantum processors remains absent. In this work, we present such a realization on IBM superconducting quantum processors. We first characterize the trade-off between adiabaticity and noise accumulation by varying the total evolution time on both the Qiskit simulator and the ibm_marrakesh processor, employing a comprehensive noise model that closely reproduces the experimental results. We then implement ideal LEO pulse derived for a closed system and achieve a significant enhancement of adiabatic fidelity within a short evolution time. To further improve the adiabatic fidelity, we refine the ideal LEO pulse via Bayesian optimization based on the comprehensive noise model. The optimized pulse yields a modest fidelity gain in simulation, yet on hardware it falls short of the ideal pulse under the present experimental conditions. Our work validates the feasibility of LEO-based adiabatic speedup on a superconducting quantum processor and highlights the potential of LEO for noise-aware adiabatic dynamics.
summary: This paper demonstrates the feasibility of achieving adiabatic speedup on superconducting quantum processors by employing Leakage Elimination Operator (LEO) control. The authors show that LEO pulses significantly boost fidelity compared to uncontrolled evolution, allowing for faster computation times while mitigating noise effects. The methodology combines theoretical control design with practical optimization techniques like Bayesian optimization, validating the approach on real quantum hardware.
main_problem: The inherent trade-off between achieving adiabatic evolution (requiring infinitely long times) and mitigating environmental noise accumulation on quantum processors.
main_result: The work validates that Leakage Elimination Operator (LEO) control can significantly enhance adiabatic fidelity, enabling speedup on superconducting quantum processors, although hardware optimization showed limitations.
method: The authors characterized the noise-adiabaticity trade-off by implementing LEO pulses, refining them via Bayesian optimization, and testing the protocol on both simulation and physical superconducting hardware.
model_or_system: The study uses superconducting quantum processors (IBM platform) to model adiabatic evolution governed by a time-dependent Hamiltonian $H(t) = H_0(t) + H_{LEO}(t)$. The dynamics are treated as an open quantum system incorporating thermal relaxation, dephasing, and depolarizing noise.
key_observables: Adiabatic fidelity ($F$), which is tracked as a function of total evolution time ($T_{tot}$) and rescaled time ($t/T_{tot}$).
important_parameters: Noise parameters ($T_1, T_2$), total evolution time ($T_{tot}$), and the number of Fourier harmonics ($K=10$) used for pulse optimization.
main_assumptions: The ideal LEO pulses are derived for closed systems, and the noise model used is a calibrated phenomenological approximation of the actual hardware noise.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the fidelity trade-off vs. time for different control methods (no control vs. LEO control) on the ibm marrakesh processor, showing fidelity peaks at an optimal, finite $T_{tot}$.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the noise-adiabaticity trade-off, then introduces and implements the LEO control, followed by optimizing the control pulse using Bayesian optimization, and finally validating the speedup on superconducting hardware.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the critical challenge of noise-aware quantum control, providing a concrete, hardware-validated technique (LEO) to accelerate quantum computations beyond the limits imposed by environmental decoherence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Simulated fidelity versus rescaled time t/Ttot for different Ttot without noise. (b) Comparison of adiabatic fidelity between simulation with a comprehensive noise model (including T1, T2 and depolarizing noise) and experimental data on the ibm marrakesh for Ttot = 1, 5, 15. For ibm marrakesh, the curves represent averages over three experimental runs, and the shaded regions indicate the error bars.

FIG. 2. Final fidelity F(t = Ttot) versus total evolution time Ttot from the ibm marrakesh processor (averages over three runs; shaded regions indicate error bars) and Qiskit simulations with a comprehensive noise model.

FIG. 3. Fidelity F versus rescaled time t/Ttot for simulations with a comprehensive noise model and the ibm marrakesh processor, at Ttot = π/2 and π, both with and without LEO control. For ibm marrakesh, curves are averages over three runs, shaded regions denote error bars.

FIG. 4. (a) Simulated fidelity F versus t/π without control, with ideal pulse control, and optimized pulse control. Inset shows the corresponding ideal and optimized LEO pulse sequences. (b) Fidelity F versus t/π on the ibm marrakesh processor with ideal and optimized LEO pulse control. Curves are averages over three experimental runs; shaded regions are error bars. Ttot = π. For the ideal pulse, I = 40, τ = π/20.
FIG. 5. Circuit diagram for a single Trotter step with LEO control.
